1: "You shall not sacrifice to the LORD your God an ox or a sheep that has a defect, any serious flaw whatever — that is detestable to the LORD your God." The unblemished sacrifice requirement. The law requires what only Christ can provide: "a lamb without blemish or spot" (1 Peter 1:19); "through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God" (Hebrews 9:14). The absolute prohibition of the defective offering positively implies the perfect offering that the law itself cannot produce from within the fallen creation. Direct type.
2: If a covenant-violator is found among you — someone who has done what is evil by transgressing the covenant. The covenant violation as the legal category. The irony of the trial of Jesus: the one who had never transgressed the covenant is processed through the covenant's legal procedure for covenant violators. The innocent one is tried as a transgressor — "he was numbered with the transgressors" (Isaiah 53:12; Luke 22:37). The legal category prepared for the guilty is the category in which the innocent one stands. Theme.
3: Serving other gods and bowing to heavenly bodies — the specific apostasy. The covenant violation that receives the capital sentence is idolatry — transferring worship from YHWH to the created order. In Christ, the opposite of this apostasy is the confession that Jesus is Lord (Philippians 2:11) — the transfer of ultimate allegiance from every created thing to the one who is both Creator and Redeemer. The apostasy Deuteronomy punishes is the failure Christ's Lordship reverses. Theme.
4: "You shall investigate diligently; and if it is true and confirmed that such a detestable thing has been done in Israel." Diligent investigation before the death sentence — no rush to execution. The standard of careful judicial procedure. Jesus' trial violates this standard: no careful investigation, accusations inconsistent and false, the sentence driven by political expediency (John 11:50: "it is better for you that one man die for the people"). The judicial standard of Deuteronomy is abused in the condemnation of Christ. Theme/contrast.
5: The covenant violator is brought to the town gate and stoned to death. The public execution at the gate. In Jesus' case: the execution outside the city gate on a hill, publicly visible. Hebrews 13:12 draws out the significance: "Jesus also suffered outside the city gate to make the people holy through his own blood" — he died in the place of public execution as the covenant violator's substitute. Direct typological application (Hebrews 13:12).
6: "A person shall be put to death only on the testimony of two or three witnesses; no one is to be executed on the testimony of a single witness." The two-or-three-witness rule protects against judicial murder. Paul applies this rule directly for church discipline (2 Corinthians 13:1; 1 Timothy 5:19). In the Sanhedrin trial, the false witnesses against Jesus could not agree (Mark 14:55–59), violating the very standard Deuteronomy requires. The innocent one is condemned in violation of the law designed to protect the innocent. Theme/contrast.
7: "The witnesses shall be first to put him to death, and then all the people shall follow." Witnesses bear primary responsibility for the execution. The covenant's judicial accountability mechanism: those who accuse must execute, owning the consequence of their testimony. In Christ's trial: the chief priests and elders drove the crowd to cry "crucify him" (Matthew 27:20–22), and the Sanhedrin bore primary responsibility. Peter's Pentecost sermon addresses this directly: "you killed the Author of life" (Acts 3:15). Revelation of God's justice.
8: Cases too difficult for local courts are brought to the central sanctuary — the Levitical priests and the judge give the final verdict. The judicial hierarchy culminates in the presence of YHWH at the sanctuary. In Christ, the supreme court is the heavenly throne where Christ sits at the Father's right hand: "We must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ" (2 Corinthians 5:10). The ascending levels of the Deuteronomic judiciary find their summit in the eschatological judgment seat of the one appointed to judge all things. Shadow.
9: "You shall go to the Levitical priests and to the judge who is serving at that time, and they shall give you the verdict." The high court's authority flows from YHWH's presence in the sanctuary. In Christ, access to the Father's authority is through the great High Priest: "we have a great high priest who has ascended into heaven, Jesus the Son of God" (Hebrews 4:14). The judge at the sanctuary is the type; Christ as the priest-judge at the heavenly sanctuary is the fulfillment. Type.
10: "You shall carry out the verdict they announce to you from that place the LORD will choose, and be careful to do all they instruct you." The supreme court's verdict is binding — obedience to the highest covenant authority. In Christ, the binding verdict is the verdict of justification: "Who shall bring any charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies. Who is to condemn? Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised — who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us" (Romans 8:33–34). Revelation of God.
11: "According to the instruction they give you and the verdict they pronounce, you shall act. Do not deviate from what they declare to you — neither to the right nor to the left." The covenant community must submit to the appointed court's judgment. In Christ, the submission to divine authority is the posture of the one who "did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself... and became obedient to the point of death" (Philippians 2:6–8). The obedience to the supreme court that Deuteronomy commands is modeled perfectly by Christ in his submission to the Father. Theme.
12: "The man who acts arrogantly by refusing to obey the priest who stands to minister before the LORD your God, or the judge — that man shall die." Presumptuous disregard for the supreme covenant authority is capital. Hebrews 10:28–29 explicitly applies this logic to Christ's superior authority: "Anyone who rejected the law of Moses died without mercy on the testimony of two or three witnesses. How much worse punishment, do you think, will be deserved by the one who has trampled underfoot the Son of God?" The escalation of authority from Moses to Christ escalates the consequence of rejection. Direct NT application (Hebrews 10:28–29).
13: "All the people shall hear and be afraid, and they will not act presumptuously again." The deterrent function of public justice — the fear of God begins with the fear of divine judgment. In Christ, the cross is the supreme demonstration of the cost of sin: "He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all" (Romans 8:32) — at the cross, the presumptuous disregard for God's authority is met by the most costly imaginable response. The cross both deters and provides escape. Revelation of God.
14: "When you come to the land... and you say, 'I want to set a king over me, like all the nations around me,'" — the law anticipates the monarchy before it exists. The permission and regulation of kingship sets up the entire Davidic covenant narrative. The king YHWH chooses is ultimately Christ, the son of David (Matthew 1:1; Acts 2:30–36): God says to him, "You are my Son; today I have begotten you" (Psalms 2:7, cited in Acts 13:33; Hebrews 1:5; Deuteronomy 5:5) — the chosen king of Deuteronomy's provision is appointed by divine declaration. Direct type.
15: "You may set a king over you — but he must be the one the LORD your God chooses... one from among your brothers." Two requirements: divine choice and brotherhood with the covenant community. Christ satisfies both: chosen by the Father ("This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased", Matthew 3:17) and truly one of his brothers ("he is not ashamed to call them brothers", Hebrews 2:11; "born of woman, born under the law", Galatians 4:4). The king from among the brothers, appointed by YHWH, is exactly who Jesus is. Direct type.
16: The king must not multiply horses or return to Egypt. The anti-accumulation provision for the king — no military self-sufficiency through cavalry power. Zechariah 9:9–10 fulfills this: the messianic king comes "humble, and mounted on a donkey" rather than a war horse, and "he will cut off the chariot from Ephraim and the war horse from Jerusalem." Jesus enters Jerusalem on a donkey (Matthew 21:5; John 12:15) — the direct fulfillment of the Zechariah prophecy that itself fulfills the Deuteronomic anti-horse provision. Direct fulfillment (Zechariah 9:9 citing Deuteronomy 17:16, fulfilled in Matthew 21:5).
17: The king must not multiply wives (lest his heart be led astray) or accumulate excessive silver and gold. Solomon violated all three provisions (1 Kgs 10–11). Christ fulfills the negative provisions: no accumulation of wives, no hoarded wealth ("foxes have holes... but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head," Matthew 8:20), no military force. The king who perfectly embodies the covenant law of the king is the one who comes in poverty, celibacy, and nonviolent authority. Type fulfilled in contrast to Solomon.
18: "When he takes the throne of his kingdom, he shall write for himself in a book a copy of this law, verified by the Levitical priests." The Torah-immersed king — the law is not external to him but personally written, carried, and read. Psalm 1, which opens the Psalter, describes the blessed person who meditates on the law day and night — a description the NT reads in relation to Christ as the one in whom Torah-meditation is perfectly realized. Christ is not merely a student of the law but is himself the living Word who is its source (John 1:1; Matthew 5:17). Shadow.
19: "It shall be with him, and he shall read in it all the days of his life, that he may learn to fear the LORD his God by keeping all the words of this law and these statutes and doing them." The king who reads the Torah daily and keeps all its words. Christ is this king: he keeps every word of the law without exception, teaches it with his own authority ("You have heard that it was said... but I say to you," Matthew 5:21–48), and fulfills it from within ("I have not come to abolish the Law or the Prophets but to fulfill them," Matthew 5:17). The Torah-keeping king of Deuteronomy is Jesus. Direct type.
20: "So that his heart will not be lifted up above his brothers, and so that he will not turn from the commandment to the right or to the left — and thus he may reign long." The humble, covenant-keeping king who does not deviate from the commandment. Christ embodies this perfectly: "he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross. Therefore God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name" (Philippians 2:8–9). The humble, commandment-keeping king of Deuteronomy receives the exaltation that follows covenant obedience. His reign is not "long" — it is eternal. Direct type.
